title: Six Pillars of Faith
subtitle: The six convictions that form the core of Islamic belief
summary: The six pillars of faith are belief in God, His angels, His revealed books, His messengers, the Last Day, and divine decree. They give Muslims a coherent view of reality: created with purpose, guided by revelation, morally accountable, and always within God's knowledge and power.

seo.metaTitle: The Six Pillars of Faith in Islam Explained
seo.metaDescription: A beginner's guide to belief in Allah, angels, revealed books, prophets, the Last Day, and divine decree, based on the Hadith of Gabriel.

sections.title: In Plain Words
sections.body:
Iman means faith or belief. In the famous Hadith of Gabriel, the Prophet Muhammad defined faith as belief in Allah, His angels, His books, His messengers, the Day of Judgment, and divine decree concerning good and evil (Sahih Muslim 8a). These became known as the six pillars of faith.

They are not six unrelated facts. Together they answer connected questions: Who made us? How does guidance reach us? Who carries divine commands? What revelation has God sent? Who models it? Where is history going? Does anything escape God's knowledge and rule? Islamic faith joins the answers into one view of a purposeful and morally serious world.

sections.title: 1. Allah and 2. His Angels
sections.body:
Belief in Allah comes first. It means affirming the one eternal Creator, His unique names and attributes, His complete knowledge and power, and His sole right to worship. Every other pillar depends on this one: angels are His servants, books are His revelation, messengers are His chosen teachers, the Last Day is His judgment, and decree belongs to His wisdom.

Angels are created beings who obey God. They are not gods, divine children, or independent forces. Islamic sources describe roles that include bringing revelation, recording deeds, taking souls at death, guarding by God's command, and carrying out tasks in the created order. Gabriel is especially known as the angel of revelation.

Belief in angels expands the believer's world beyond what the senses can measure, but it does not authorize fantasy. Muslims affirm what reliable revelation teaches and avoid confident stories about the unseen without evidence.

sections.title: 3. His Books and 4. His Messengers
sections.body:
Quran 2:285 joins belief in God, angels, books, and messengers. Muslims believe God revealed guidance before the Quran, including the Torah given to Moses, the Psalms given to David, and the Gospel given to Jesus. The Quran also mentions pages or scrolls associated with Abraham and Moses. Respect for those original revelations is part of faith.

Islam distinguishes the original revelation from every later manuscript, translation, interpretation, or canon attributed to it. Muslim views about the transmission of earlier scriptures require more careful discussion than the slogan that everything was simply lost or rewritten. The foundational point here is positive: God has guided humanity repeatedly, and Muslims must honor the true revelation He sent.

Messengers deliver and embody that guidance. Muslims believe in Abraham, Moses, Jesus, Muhammad, and all God's true prophets. They do not worship them or accept some while rejecting others out of prejudice. Muhammad is understood as the final prophet, and the Quran as the final revealed book governing Muslim belief and practice.

sections.title: 5. The Last Day
sections.body:
Belief in the Last Day means that earthly death is not the end of human existence. God will raise creation, expose what was hidden, judge with perfect knowledge and justice, and grant final consequences. The Quran speaks of resurrection, the gathering, the record of deeds, judgment, Paradise, and Hell. Quran 4:136 includes the Last Day among the essential objects of faith.

This belief gives moral weight to choices that human courts never see. An oppressed person is not forgotten, and a powerful wrongdoer does not escape merely by dying with a good reputation. It also prevents ethics from becoming only fear: God's mercy, forgiveness, and acceptance of repentance are central to the believer's hope. Muslims are taught to work, repair harm, and seek forgiveness rather than speculate obsessively about the exact date of the end.

sections.title: 6. Divine Decree
sections.body:
Qadar, or divine decree, means that nothing lies outside God's knowledge, will, and creative power. Quran 54:49 states that all things were created with decree. God knows the whole of reality without learning it in stages, and events do not surprise or overpower Him.

This does not turn human beings into morally meaningless puppets. The Quran addresses people as choosing, intending, obeying, disobeying, repenting, and bearing responsibility. Sunni schools have developed different technical explanations of how divine creation and human agency fit together. Ashari theology is known for the idea that human beings acquire acts God creates, while Maturidi theology often gives a more explicit account of created human capacity; Athari scholars tend to stay closer to the scriptural affirmations without extensive speculation. All reject both an independent human power outside God's rule and a fatalism that erases responsibility.

Practically, belief in decree produces a balance. A Muslim plans, chooses, works, prays, and accepts consequences, because effort is itself part of the created order. After doing what is right and possible, the believer does not imagine that a missed opportunity escaped God's knowledge. Qadar is not an excuse for sin or laziness; it is a source of humility in success and steadiness in loss.

sections.title: Faith That Shapes Action
sections.body:
The six pillars describe convictions of the heart, but Islamic faith is meant to appear in speech and action. Belief in God leads to worship; belief in angels encourages integrity when no person is watching; belief in books creates a duty to learn; belief in messengers invites imitation; belief in judgment strengthens accountability; and belief in decree gives courage without arrogance.

Sunni scholars have used different formulations when explaining the relationship between faith and deeds. They agree that deeds matter deeply and that open disobedience harms the believer. Beginners should avoid using theological vocabulary as a weapon for declaring other Muslims outside the faith. The Hadith of Gabriel itself places Islam, Iman, and excellence within one religion and ends by explaining that Gabriel came to teach the community its way of life.

quranVerses.arabic: ءَامَنَ ٱلرَّسُولُ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِن رَّبِّهِۦ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ۚ كُلٌّ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَمَلَٰٓئِكَتِهِۦ وَكُتُبِهِۦ وَرُسُلِهِۦ لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍۢ مِّن رُّسُلِهِۦ ۚ وَقَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا ۖ غُفْرَانَكَ رَبَّنَا وَإِلَيْكَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ
quranVerses.translation: The Messenger has believed in what was revealed to him from his Lord, and [so have] the believers. All of them have believed in Allah and His angels and His books and His messengers, [saying], "We make no distinction between any of His messengers." And they say, "We hear and we obey. [We seek] Your forgiveness, our Lord, and to You is the [final] destination."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: يَٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ وَٱلْكِتَٰبِ ٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ عَلَىٰ رَسُولِهِۦ وَٱلْكِتَٰبِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ مِن قَبْلُ ۚ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِٱللَّهِ وَمَلَٰٓئِكَتِهِۦ وَكُتُبِهِۦ وَرُسُلِهِۦ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْءَاخِرِ فَقَدْ ضَلَّ ضَلَٰلًۢا بَعِيدًا
quranVerses.translation: O you who have believed, believe in Allah and His Messenger and the Book that He sent down upon His Messenger and the Scripture which He sent down before. And whoever disbelieves in Allah, His angels, His books, His messengers, and the Last Day has certainly gone far astray.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.translation: Indeed, all things We created with predestination.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

faq.question: Where does the list of six pillars come from?
faq.answer: The classical list is stated in the Hadith of Gabriel, recorded as Sahih Muslim 8a. Quran passages such as 2:285, 4:136, and 54:49 teach the same components across several verses.

faq.question: Do Muslims believe in earlier revealed books?
faq.answer: Yes. Muslims affirm the Torah given to Moses, the Psalms given to David, the Gospel given to Jesus, and other revelation named by the Quran. Questions about how later textual traditions relate to those original revelations require careful historical and theological study.

faq.question: Does belief in qadar mean choices do not matter?
faq.answer: No. Islam affirms both God's complete decree and genuine human accountability. A person must choose, act, repent, and repair harm; qadar cannot be used as an excuse for wrongdoing or neglect.

faq.question: Are angels like human spirits or lesser gods?
faq.answer: Neither. Angels are a distinct created order that obeys God and performs assigned tasks. They do not share divinity and are not worshipped.

furtherReading.title: The Cambridge Companion to Classical Islamic Theology
furtherReading.author: Tim Winter, editor
furtherReading.note: A scholarly survey of Sunni theological history and major doctrines, published by Cambridge University Press.

furtherReading.title: Islam: A Very Short Introduction
furtherReading.author: Malise Ruthven
furtherReading.note: A concise orientation to Islamic beliefs, institutions, and diversity, published by Oxford University Press.
